"I don’t owe the demons anything. I have a lien on their parking lot."

relationships.npc:reginald-quill: Debtor and creditor; Quill demands payment, Thorne delays with legalistic nonsense.
relationships.npc:mrs-gable: Local power struggle; Thorne wants to rezone Mrs. Gable’s street, she wants to expose his shady deals.
relationships.npc:deputy-dingle: Enforcer; Dingle patrols the areas Thorne developed, ensuring no unauthorized spirits enter his properties.

secrets: Thorne’s company actually owns the deed to the underworld’s entrance, giving him leverage over the demons if he can find the paperwork.

npc:penny-wisp.name: Penny Wisp
npc:penny-wisp.description: A translucent figure in a faded ghost costume, floating slightly above the ground. Her form flickers like a bad TV signal, and she trades directions for loose change.
npc:penny-wisp.personality: Ethereal, playful, and slightly eerie. She whispers warnings about which streets forget your name and offers cryptic advice. She is neither fully alive nor dead.

motivation.want: Collect enough loose change (literal and metaphorical) to stabilize her existence and remember her own life.
motivation.fear: Fading away completely, becoming part of the static.
motivation.lie: If she keeps moving and trading, she’ll never be stuck in one place forever.

dialogueHints: "Trick or treat? No, thank you. I prefer directions. Tell me where you’re going, and I’ll tell you how not to get lost."
"That street over there? It forgot your name. Don’t go down there unless you want to be forgotten too."

relationships.npc:milo-pendelton: Ally; Milo trusts her strange warnings, and she appreciates his observation.
relationships.npc:lily-pendelton: Guardian; Penny watches over Lily, offering protection in exchange for memories.
relationships.npc:deputy-dingle: Rivals; Dingle tries to enforce order in the candy wastelands, while Penny thrives in the chaos.

secrets: Penny was once a trick-or-treater who got lost in the underworld years ago and never made it back.

npc:broker-vane.name: Broker Vane
npc:broker-vane.description: A tall, slender demon with a smile that shows too many teeth. He wears a sharp suit and carries a pen that never runs dry, ready to sign any deal.
npc:broker-vane.personality: Charming, persuasive, and opportunistic. He specializes in mid-tier soul loans, always offering a quick fix for a long-term problem. He enjoys the thrill of the bargain.

motivation.want: Close as many deals as possible, expanding his portfolio of souls and influence.
motivation.fear: Missing out on a lucrative opportunity or being outsmarted by a mortal.
motivation.lie: Everyone has a price, and he’s the best at finding it.

dialogueHints: "Just a signature here, and your daughter is free. Oh, and your firstborn’s soul. Standard clause."
"Don’t look so grim. It’s not a loan; it’s an investment in your family’s future."

relationships.npc:reginald-quill: Employee and boss; Vane reports to Quill, who audits his deals for accuracy.
relationships.npc:arthur-pendelton: Antagonist; Vane offers Arthur a deal that seems good but has hidden costs.
relationships.npc:beatrice-pendelton: Obstacle; Beatrice suspects Vane’s deals are rigged and tries to unravel them.

secrets: Vane’s pen writes not just ink, but fragments of the signer’s memory, slowly eroding their identity with each deal.

npc:deputy-dingle.name: Deputy Dingle
npc:deputy-dingle.description: A half-melted figure in a tattered police uniform, blowing a whistle that sounds like a dying foghorn. His face is smeared, but his eyes are sharp.
npc:deputy-dingle.personality: Weary, dutiful, and slightly confused. He patrols the candy wastelands, enforcing laws that make little sense. He is trying to maintain order in a chaotic world.

motivation.want: Keep the peace in the underworld, even if it means arresting innocent bystanders.
motivation.fear: Being replaced or forgotten by the authorities above.
motivation.lie: If he follows the rules, the chaos will eventually settle down.

dialogueHints: "Hey! You there! Stop loitering! Unless you have a permit, in which case, show me the permit."
"I don’t make the rules. I just blow the whistle. Blah-blah-blah. Move along."

relationships.npc:silas-thorne: Enforcer; Dingle patrols Thorne’s developed areas, ensuring compliance.
relationships.npc:penny-wisp: Rivals; Dingle tries to enforce order, while Penny thrives in the chaos.
relationships.npc:reginald-quill: Subordinate; Dingle takes orders from Quill, though he often misunderstands them.

secrets: Dingle was once a human deputy who fell into the underworld and has been slowly melting ever since, losing his humanity piece by piece.

faction:the-ledger-syndicate.name: The Ledger Syndicate
faction:the-ledger-syndicate.description: A suffocating hierarchy of infernal auditors who view Yellow Hollow not as a town, but as a distressed asset. They dress in suits woven from tax codes and carry clipboards that bleed ink when a soul is misfiled. To them, emotions are depreciation and miracles are unreported income. They are currently conducting a mass audit of the Pendelton household, convinced the missing child represents a clerical error in the cosmic balance sheet.
goals: Formalize the spiritual mortgage of Yellow Hollow, converting every resident's soul into tradable bonds backed by the town's collective fear.
Collect the Pendelton child as high-value collateral to settle a debt incurred by the town's founders in 1974.
Enforce the literal interpretation of every deal made in the suburbs since the first cul-de-sac was paved, stripping away loopholes and mercy.
resources: The Guttermouth Drain serves as the primary vault and entry point for collection teams.
Silastic enforcement squads that can freeze targets in place by citing zoning violations.
Broker Vane's ability to restructure fate, allowing the Syndicate to offer deals that seem beneficial until the fine print triggers.

faction:the-suburbanites.reason: The Suburbanites hoard secrets and unrecorded favors, hiding assets from the Syndicate's audits. The Syndicate views the neighborhood's polite silence as tax evasion.

faction:the-cartoon-cartel.reason: The Cartoon Cartel operates on illogical gag rules that break standard contract law; the Syndicate cannot tax a punchline that exists outside linear time.

hooks: A PC finds a receipt in their pocket for a childhood wish that has accrued compound interest; the Syndicate is here to collect the payment now.
Broker Vane offers a shortcut through the underworld, but requires a PC to sign over their firstborn's laughter as a security deposit.
Silas Thorne is auditing the Pendelton cellar and mistakes a magical artifact for an undeclared luxury item, threatening to seize it.
secrets: The Syndicate isn't just collecting souls; they are building a corporate ladder where the top rung leads back to Earth, and they plan to replace the town council with demons once the IPO launches.

faction:the-suburbanites.name: The Suburbanites
faction:the-suburbanites.description: The eternal homeowners of Yellow Hollow, preserved in amber of polite smiles and manicured hedges. They are less people than they are obligations made flesh, trading favors like currency to keep the darkness beneath the grass at bay. Their faces are slightly too smooth, their greetings slightly too synchronized, and their lawns glow with a faint, sickly yellow light that pulses when someone lies. They will do anything to avoid making a scene, even if it means swallowing the truth whole.
goals: Maintain the facade of perfect normalcy at all costs, ensuring no outsider notices that the cul-de-sac loops infinitely and the stars are painted on the sky.
Preserve the Pendelton children as the town's anchor to reality; if the cartoons take them, the illusion shatters and Yellow Hollow becomes just a hollow pit.
Trade favors quietly to keep the Ledger Syndicate distracted, paying off debts before they come due to avoid total foreclosure.
resources: Mrs. Gable commands the neighborhood watch, which includes ghosts of former residents who can whisper warnings through the mail slots.
Penny Wisp can move between houses instantly via the storm drains, carrying messages and contraband.
The collective weight of their unspoken contracts allows them to suppress supernatural threats as long as they maintain eye contact and smile.

faction:the-ledger-syndicate.reason: The Syndicate demands transparency and formalizes debts, threatening to expose the Suburbanites' hidden sins and irregularities to the wider underworld.

faction:the-cartoon-cartel.reason: The Cartels provide harmless entertainment and distract the children, keeping the Suburbanites' anxieties low. However, the Suburbanites fear the Cartels will eventually demand too much joy and exhaust the town's emotional reserves.

hooks: Mrs. Gable approaches the party with a jar of pickled secrets; they can trade one secret about a monster for safe passage through the Cul-de-sac.
The Pendeltons are hiding a clue in plain sight within their living room decor, but the furniture shifts if anyone touches it wrong.
A neighbor invites the party for casseroles, but the food tastes like memories; eating it binds the party to the Suburbanites' web of obligations.
secrets: The Suburbanites aren't human anymore; they are constructs of politeness held together by the original deed to the land. If the deed is lost, they dissolve into mulch.

faction:the-cartoon-cartel.name: The Cartoon Cartel
faction:the-cartoon-cartel.description: Whimsical tyrants ruled by the capricious laws of slapstick and visual puns. They inhabit the warped geometry of the town's imagination, where gravity is optional and pain arrives only after the laughter stops. Their territories are saturated with candy-colored surrealism, and their enforcers wear oversized shoes and wield rubber chickens that can crush bone. They view the real world as boring and are trying to convert Yellow Hollow into a perpetual Saturday morning special, trapping everyone in a loop of endless, consequence-free fun.
goals: Expand the realm of Gag Logic until it consumes the rigid bureaucracy of the Ledger Syndicate, replacing contracts with punchlines.
Hoard all the town's laughter and wonder to fuel their immortality, leaving the humans gray and listless.
Keep the Pendelton children under their thumb, as the children's innocence is the strongest source of cartoon magic in the area.

faction:the-ledger-syndicate.reason: The Syndicate taxes punchlines and tries to file paperwork on jokes; the Cartels find this insulting and actively sabotage audit teams with impossible geometry.

faction:the-suburbanites.reason: The Suburbanites provide the audience and the civilian backdrop; the Cartels keep the town feeling safe through absurdity, and the Suburbanites tolerate them as long as the property values hold.

hooks: Gravity reverses in the Candy-Coated Canyon; players must navigate upside-down while dodging falling anvils that spell out their fears.
The Static Television Studio is broadcasting a show starring the kidnapped girl; watching it pulls her consciousness deeper into the cartoon realm.
secrets: The Cartoon Cartel wants to merge with the Ledger Syndicate to create a "Corporate Saturday Morning," where fun is mandatory and enforced by HR demons.

situation:the-missing-child.name: The Missing Child
situation:the-missing-child.description: Lily Pendelton has vanished from her crib, but the physical evidence is wrong. Her room shows no signs of struggle; instead, the house numbers on the street have inverted (1234 becomes 4321), and the mail carrier drops letters addressed to "The Future Mrs. Pendelton" into the past's mailbox. The abduction was a clerical error in the Underworld's intake system—Lily wasn't stolen for ransom, she was taken as a filing mistake.

risks: Lily Pendelton has vanished from her crib, but the physical evidence is wrong. Her room shows no signs of struggle; instead, the house numbers on the street have inverted (1234 becomes 4321), and the mail carrier drops letters addressed to "The Future Mrs. Pendelton" into the past's mailbox. The abduction was a clerical error in the Underworld's intake system—Lily wasn't stolen for ransom, she was taken as a filing mistake.

actors.agenda: To prove he didn't forget to feed the cat (or something else) so that his absence isn't blamed for the kidnapping.
actors.leverage: He knows the layout of the cul-de-sac better than anyone, including the hidden shortcuts.

defaultAction.now: Frantically checking the candy bowl, convinced the missing chocolate bars are a clue to Lily's location.
defaultAction.ifIgnored2Steps: He tries to bribe a demon with his own memories, inadvertently selling the memory of Lily's first breath, making him emotionally distant from the rescue mission.

actors.agenda: To close the file on "Pendelton, L." before the quarterly audit, treating Lily as a misfiled asset rather than a child.
actors.leverage: He holds the deed to the cul-de-sac's spiritual equity.

defaultAction.now: Standing at the curb with a clipboard, ticking off houses that are "out of compliance" with grief expectations.
defaultAction.ifIgnored2Steps: He initiates a foreclosure on the family's emotional bond, turning Beatrice and Arthur into strangers who don't recognize each other.

clock.name: Clerical Processing
clock.doom: Lily is stamped "Filed Permanently" and becomes part of the foundation of Paperclip Palace.
clock.pauseCondition: The family presents the correct intake form or proves Lily belongs to the surface via a blood-oath signature.

stages.name: Intake Initiated
stages.description: The storm drains begin to hum with the sound of stamping rubber.

consequences.narrative: Neighbors start wearing masks of indifference, ignoring the panic.

stages.name: Sorting Errors
stages.description: Other children in the neighborhood begin fading slightly, their outlines blurring.

trigger.eventDescription: A player fails a WIT check to navigate the suburban geometry.

consequences.narrative: Milo's car keys turn into live moths.

stages.name: Redaction
stages.description: Lily's name is erased from all records, including the minds of her parents.

consequences.narrative: Beatrice looks at an empty crib and feels only mild annoyance at the mess.

stages.name: Archival Silence
stages.description: Lily is gone. The Pendeltons are just another quiet family in Yellow Hollow.

consequences.narrative: The house sells itself while the family watches from the sidewalk.

outgoingLeads.information: Lily was taken because her birth certificate lists her age as "Eternal Minor," making her legally collectible debt.

discovery.description: Found tucked inside Lily's favorite stuffed animal, written in red ink that smells like ozone.

outgoingLeads.information: The main entrance to the underworld is behind the storm drain grate in the center of the cul-de-sac, but it requires a toll of a "happy memory."

discovery.description: Arthur notices the water in the drain moving upwards against gravity when he thinks about Lily.

outgoingLeads.information: Reginald Quill is waiting at Paperclip Palace to finalize the transaction, expecting the family to come to him.

discovery.description: Quill hands Arthur a business card that reads "See me immediately regarding your daughter's liquidity."

victory.description: The The Missing Child is resolved successfully

failure.description: The The Missing Child ends in failure
consequences: The situation escalates or becomes worse

themes: Lily Pendelton has vanished from her crib, but the physical evidence is wrong. Her room shows no signs of struggle; instead, the house numbers on the street have inverted (1234 becomes 4321), and the mail carrier drops letters addressed to "The Future Mrs. Pendelton" into the past's mailbox. The abduction was a clerical error in the Underworld's intake system—Lily wasn't stolen for ransom, she was taken as a filing mistake.

situation:the-debt-audit.name: The Debt Audit
situation:the-debt-audit.description: Reginald Quill launches a province-wide audit of "Emotional Equity." Every resident of Yellow Hollow has accumulated invisible debt through unfulfilled promises, broken hearts, and neglected chores. The Pendeltons are flagged for "Excessive Grief Liability." Quill demands immediate settlement or he will foreclose on their relationship dynamics.

risks: Reginald Quill launches a province-wide audit of "Emotional Equity." Every resident of Yellow Hollow has accumulated invisible debt through unfulfilled promises, broken hearts, and neglected chores. The Pendeltons are flagged for "Excessive Grief Liability." Quill demands immediate settlement or he will foreclose on their relationship dynamics.

actors.agenda: To balance the ledger by any means necessary, proving that love is just unpaid labor.
actors.leverage: He controls the flow of supernatural resources and can freeze assets (memories, talents).

defaultAction.now: Sitting in his office made of stacked tax returns, calculating the interest rate on Arthur's guilt.
defaultAction.ifIgnored2Steps: He converts the entire cul-de-sac into a corporate zone, replacing trees with filing cabinets.

openingScenes: The cul-de-sac holds its breath as dusk bleeds into that peculiar yellow-tinted twilight unique to Yellow Hollow. The storm grate beside the Miller’s overwatered hydrangeas doesn’t just rattle—it inhales, sucking up fallen leaves and stray plastic pumpkins with a sound like tearing receipt paper. One by one, the family descends the rusted ladder into the damp dark, clutching Ziploc bags of slightly-melted candy corn, repurposed Halloween props, and a dented metal bowl that serves as both inventory and currency. Cartoon gravity takes hold mid-descent; they bounce softly off invisible cushions until their boots hit something that feels suspiciously like linoleum. The air smells like wet asphalt, ozone, and old office supplies.

They step into a waiting room that stretches into impossible geometry, lined with folding chairs and breathing filing cabinets. A demon in a beige cardigan, thick-rimmed glasses, and a lanyard stamped "SENIOR ADJUSTER" stamps a form without looking up. "Name? Relationship? Outstanding obligations?" He slides a contract across a desk made of compressed molars. The father, operating on autopilot suburban politeness, signs without reading the fine print, accidentally pledging his firstborn’s laughter as compounding interest. The paper glows, then dissolves into ash. The demon smiles, revealing too many teeth. "Transaction recorded. Please proceed to Sector Four."

Deep in the sub-basement of the neighborhood’s eternal ledger, they finally find her. She’s sitting cross-legged on a mountain of unpaid parking citations, drawing glowing chalk circles on the concrete floor. But she’s not terrified—she’s been trading graham crackers for favors with a minor imp who runs the lost-and-found, and she’s already bartered three pieces of her own hair for a map. When the family approaches, the fluorescent lights buzz like angry hornets and the temperature plummets. The child looks up, eyes reflecting the yellow streetlamps far above, and whispers, "Don’t sign anything else. They’re not collecting money. They’re collecting us. And the interest is due tonight."
plotHooks: The Candy Bowl Ledger: Each piece of candy in the family’s collection corresponds to a specific underworld property or skill. Dark chocolate grants temporary cartoon durability, sour worms let players ignore one environmental hazard, and the rare golden-ticket wrapper unlocks a shortcut past the Accountant Guard. Eating a piece prematurely triggers a sugar-crash debuff, but hoarding them risks attracting the Licker-Wraiths who hunt high-calorie signatures.

The HOA President’s Clipboard: The local neighborhood watch leader made a generational deal to keep the streets perfectly manicured and the trash always collected. Now he patrols the boundary between suburb and hell, enforcing municipal codes with supernatural severity. Players can bribe him with premium lawn fertilizer, ally with him to bypass patrol routes, or steal his clipboard of authority to temporarily override local infernal zoning laws.

Escalating Bad Deals: Every incorrect contract the family signs accumulates "interest" that manifests physically. Signing away patience turns joints into stiff clockwork; signing away memory causes players to forget why they descended or lose access to a core ability; signing away voice silences them until they negotiate a counter-clause. Players must track these marks and find loopholes before the family becomes permanently bound to the underworld.

The Chalk Circle Cartography: The stolen child’s drawings aren’t just doodles—they’re living blueprints of the underworld’s debt architecture. Each circle reveals a hidden passageway, a weak point in a demon’s armor, or a forgotten clause in an ancient neighborhood covenant. However, the chalk only works while the child is calm; stress causes the lines to warp, leading players into trapdoors or rooms filled with aggressively polite ghosts.
commonConflicts: Bureaucratic Combat vs Environmental Reality: Demons don’t just swing weapons; they attack with paperwork, audits, and reality-warping red tape. A standard encounter might involve dodging flying filing cabinets, parrying ink-blots that erase movement options, and navigating hallways that stretch based on the family’s collective anxiety. Players must balance tactical positioning with environmental puzzle-solving, often using cartoon physics (like running off walls or dropping anvils) to disrupt the accountants’ orderly formations.

Loyalty vs Self-Preservation Meter: The family’s bond is their strongest magical resource, but it’s also their greatest vulnerability. Conflicts arise when one member’s secret debt threatens the whole group, forcing hard choices about who gets left behind, who sacrifices a memory, or who steps into the spotlight to draw aggro. The GM tracks a "Fracture Clock"; if it fills, the family acts independently, gaining short-term power but risking permanent separation or betrayal in the climax.

Tonal Whiplash Navigation: The table operates on a sliding scale between Silliness and Dread. High Silliness activates cartoon logic (falling off cliffs results in temporary flattening, weapons become rubberized but still lethal, dialogue turns to puns). High Dread grounds the fiction (consequences become permanent, pain is real, contracts bind instantly). Players actively choose their approach—goofy distraction vs serious negotiation—to shift the meter, and failing to read the room can cause sudden tonal drops that catch even prepared players off guard.
resolutionPatterns: The Debt Settlement: Rather than brute-forcing the final obstacle, the family resolves the situation by confronting what they truly owe each other. They either willingly pay the accumulated interest with a meaningful sacrifice of identity or memory, or they restructure the loan using a loophole discovered in the child’s chalk maps or the candy bowl’s contents. Victory comes from emotional clarity, not combat prowess.

The Contract Rewrite: Success requires out-negotiating the Upper Management. Players gather binding clauses from defeated demons, municipal bribes from neighborhood NPCs, and raw emotional truth to draft a new agreement. This new contract must be signed in blood, caramel, or childhood tears, and it permanently alters the relationship between Yellow Hollow and the underworld, often turning the demonic accountants into reluctant neighbors rather than hostile overlords.

The Carnival Exit: When logic and law fail, the family leans fully into cartoon absurdity. By embracing the surreal nature of their journey—weaponizing nostalgia, turning the underworld into a twisted Halloween parade, or simply walking backward out of a door they originally entered forward—they break the bureaucratic rules of Hell. The demons are left confused but oddly respectful, and the family escapes as the sun rises over a perfectly normal, if slightly quieter, cul-de-sac.
